coredata.dc:description: Zinc selenide (ZnSe) is a next-generation anode material due to its high sodium storage capacity. However, the poor conductivity and volume expansion lead to its capacity attenuation and short cycle life. Herein, Se-vacancy porous ultrathin ZnSe nanosheet/carbon composite (ZnSe@NC/N-GQD) is reported as a high-performance anode material for sodium-ion batteries. Organic-inorganic hybrid ZnSe(en)0.5 ultrathin nanosheets with abundant Se vacancies are rapidly prepared by electron beam irradiation for the first time. Using it as a precursor, Se-vacancy carbon-coated porous ultrathin ZnSe nanosheets loaded with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are synthesized via carbonization and electrodeposition process. Through the design of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD, the sodium storage performance can be effectively improved by implementing nanostructure engineering (porous ultrathin nanosheet) via organic–inorganic hybrid, defect engineering (Se vacancy) via electron beam irradiation, and carbon composite (amorphous carbon and N-GQD). Consequently, ZnSe@NC/N-GQD delivers a high capacity of 483 mA h g−1 after 200 cycles at 0.5 A g−1, shows an outstanding rate capability of 381 mA h g−1 at 10.0 A g−1, and exhibits an excellent cycling stability of 86 % retention after 2800 cycles at 5.0 A g−1. This work develops a new method to rapidly prepare organic–inorganic nanohybrids, and proposes an innovative design of high-performance ZnSe-based anodes.

head.ce:title: Se-vacancy porous ultrathin ZnSe nanosheet/carbon composite for sodium storage via electron beam irradiation strategy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zinc selenide (ZnSe) is a next-generation anode material due to its high sodium storage capacity. However, the poor conductivity and volume expansion lead to its capacity attenuation and short cycle life. Herein, Se-vacancy porous ultrathin ZnSe nanosheet/carbon composite (ZnSe@NC/N-GQD) is reported as a high-performance anode material for sodium-ion batteries. Organic-inorganic hybrid ZnSe(en)0.5 ultrathin nanosheets with abundant Se vacancies are rapidly prepared by electron beam irradiation for the first time. Using it as a precursor, Se-vacancy carbon-coated porous ultrathin ZnSe nanosheets loaded with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are synthesized via carbonization and electrodeposition process. Through the design of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD, the sodium storage performance can be effectively improved by implementing nanostructure engineering (porous ultrathin nanosheet) via organic–inorganic hybrid, defect engineering (Se vacancy) via electron beam irradiation, and carbon composite (amorphous carbon and N-GQD). Consequently, ZnSe@NC/N-GQD delivers a high capacity of 483 mA h g−1 after 200 cycles at 0.5 A g−1, shows an outstanding rate capability of 381 mA h g−1 at 10.0 A g−1, and exhibits an excellent cycling stability of 86 % retention after 2800 cycles at 5.0 A g−1. This work develops a new method to rapidly prepare organic–inorganic nanohybrids, and proposes an innovative design of high-performance ZnSe-based anodes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) have attracted much attention as a promising alternative to lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) due to their low cost and the abundance of resources [1–3]. However, the larger radius of sodium ion (1.02
Å
and 0.76
Å
for sodium ion and lithium ion, respectively) results in poor reaction kinetics, and structure deformation during charging and discharging, which is not conducive to long-term stability [4]. Therefore, designing anode materials with the capability of enhancing charge transfer kinetics and alleviating volume change represents a pivotal stage in the advancement of SIBs.

As a typical conversion material, ZnSe has the advantages of appropriate REDOX reaction potential, high theoretical capacity, good thermal stability, and abundant resources [5]. However, the poor conductivity and huge volume expansion lead to its capacity attenuation and short cycle life [6,7]. To address these issues, many researchers have implemented some strategies, including carbon composites, defect engineering, and the construction of nanostructures [8–10]. The conductivity and structure stability of ZnSe can be effectively enhanced by compositing with carbon materials [11]. In addition, defect engineering also represents another strategy to improve sodium storage performance. Se vacancy can provide numerous active sites, sodium ion adsorption and storage sites, which improves the reaction kinetics [12,13]. Moreover, the Se vacancy changes the electronic structure of the selenides and thus enhances the electrical conductivity [14,15].

The solvothermal method is the most common method for preparing organic–inorganic hybrid nanosheets [31]. However, high reaction temperature (100 ∼ 200℃), long reaction time (12 ∼ 120 h) and the use of toxic reducing agents (e. g. hydrazine hydrate) make the solvothermal method unsuitable for large-scale synthesis [27,30,32–34]. Among many new methods for the preparation of nanomaterials, electron beam irradiation (EBI) has gradually shown its unique advantages [35]. Under the action of high-energy electrons, nanocrystals can nucleate rapidly to prepare ultra-small-sized nanomaterials, such as ultrathin nanosheets. Furthermore, ultrathin nanosheets can be synthesized within only a few minutes at room temperature and constant pressure without any kind of toxic reduction reagents. More importantly, Se vacancies can be achieved by high-energy electrons. Compared with other chemical methods, EBI can precisely regulate Se vacancies without introducing any impurities. Therefore, using these advantages of electron beam irradiation, a new avenue for rapid and large-scale synthesis of organic–inorganic hybrid selenide ultrathin nanosheets at normal temperature and pressure is explored. It is worth noting that, to the best of our knowledge, there is no research report on the construction of porous ultrathin nanosheets with Se vacancies.

Herein, we firstly report that organic–inorganic hybrid ZnSe(en)0.5 ultrathin nanosheets with Se vacancies are rapidly prepared by electron beam irradiation. ZnSe(en)0.5 as the precursor is carbonized to synthesize carbon-coated porous ultrathin ZnSe nanosheets with Se vacancy (ZnSe@NC). Finally, N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are loaded on ZnSe@NC by electrodeposition to fabricate ZnSe@NC/N-GQD, which further improves the Na+ storage performance of ZnSe. The distinctive nanostructure of porous ultrathin nanosheet enables remarkable Na+ diffusion kinetics and cycling stability. The presence of Se vacancies serves to enhance a number of active sites available for Na+ storage. In-situ grown nitrogen-doped amorphous carbon and introducing N-GQDs improve electrical conductivity and accelerate reaction kinetics. Consequently, as an anode material for SIBs, ZnSe@NC/N-GQD exhibits a specific capacity of 483 mA h g−1 after 200 cycles at 0.5 A g−1, and a rate capacity of 381 mA h g−1 at 10.0 A g−1. In particular, the capacity remains at 86 % of the specific capacity at 5.0 A g−1 after 2800 cycles. In this work, a novel avenue for constructing defective porous ultrathin nanosheets is proposed via the strategies of organic–inorganic hybrid and electron beam irradiation, and a new method for rapid and large-scale preparation of organic–inorganic hybrid nanomaterials is developed by electron beam irradiation.

The ZnSe(en)0.5 was prepared by electron beam irradiation method. Firstly, 1 mmol of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 50 mL of ethylenediamine (en) were added into 50 ml of deionized water in sequence, stirring until the solid is dissolved. Subsequently, 1 mmol of Na2SeO3 and 6 mL of isopropanol (IPA) were added to the above-mixed solution, respectively, and stirred for 1 h until the solution was clear and solid-free. Then, 25 ml mixed solution was sealed into a high-temperature cooking bag. The irradiation experiment was carried out using an electron beam accelerator (GJ-2-Ⅱ, Shanghai Xianfeng Power Plant). The working voltage was 2 MeV, and the single channel beam was 10 mA. 10 kGy irradiation was supplied each pass, and the total irradiation was controlled by the number of passes. The irradiation duration of about 30 min to control the irradiation doses of 350 kGy. After irradiation, the sample was sedimented using a high-speed centrifuge at a centrifugal speed of 8000 r min−1 for 5 min, and the sediment was centrally washed 3 times using 70 % ethanol solution. Finally, the sediment was placed into the vacuum dryer at 60 °C and dried for 12 h to obtain the powder sample.
The ZnSe(en)0.5 precursor was placed in the N2 atmosphere and pyrolysis carbonization at 600℃ for 4 h to obtain the ZnSe@NC composite.

The N-GQDs were synthesized via the bottom-up approach [36]. First, a 12.5 g L-1 solution of pyrene nitrate was configured and stirred at 80 °C for 48 h. After the reaction, the precursor was washed with deionized water and dried at 60 °C for 24 h to obtain the precursor. Next, a 10 g L-1 aqueous solution was configured and 1/10 vol% hydrazine hydrate was added. After 12 h of hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C, the N-GQD solution was obtained by multiple filtrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnSe@NC and ZnSe@NC/N-GQD electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The working electrode consists of ZnSe@NC, carbon black, and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) with a weight ratio of 8: 1: 1 in N-methyl pyrrolidone diluter, evenly mixed and coated on the copper foil, and put in a vacuum oven to dry at 70℃ for 12 h to obtain ZnSe@NC electrode.

To obtain ZnSe@NC/N-GQD, the ZnSe@NC electrode was clamped on the working electrode, and platinum wire was used as the counter electrode. The N-GQDs were deposited on ZnSe@NC by electrodeposition. After washing and drying, the electrode was annealed at 500 °C for 1 h under 5 % H2/Ar atmosphere to obtain the ZnSe@NC/N-GQD electrode. Finally, the electrode was cut into 10 mm diameter circular. The load of the active substance on a single electrode sheet was about 0.8 ∼ 1.1 mg cm−2.

The crystalline structures of samples were measured with X-ray diffractometer (XRD, 3 kw D/MAX-2200 V PC) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) in the angle of 2θ = 5-80°. The thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA, STA449C) was conducted in a nitrogen atmosphere, with the temperature increasing from 50℃ to 1200℃ at a rate of 10℃ min−1. The morphology of the samples was characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F) and atomic force microscope (AFM, Dimension Icon). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, ESCALAB250Xi) was employed to ascertain the element properties. Raman spectrum (inVia Qontor) was conducted to analyze the carbon structures of the composites. The presence of selenium vacancy defects in the materials was verified by electronic paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (EPR, EMXplus-6/1). The pore sizes and surface area of the sample were evaluated using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method with N2 as the adsorbate gas.

The electrochemical tests were conducted by assembling coin-type half-cells (CR2032). Sodium was used as the counter electrode, sodium hexafluorophosphate (NaPF6) was dissolved in diethylene glycol dimethyl ether (DIG) configured into 1.0 M electrolyte, and Whatman GF-D glass fiber was used as the separator. The configuration of the electrolyte and the assembly of the battery were carried out in an argon-filled glove box with less than 0.01 ppm oxygen/water content. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests were carried out on the Autolab (PGSTAT128N) electrochemical workstation within the voltage range of 0.1–3.0 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were carried out on the Autolab electrochemical workstation at the same voltage window in the frequency range of 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz. Galvanostatic discharge/charge cycles and electrochemical performance were performed on the LAND CT2001A battery tester in a voltage range of 0.1–3.0 V at room temperature.

The synthetic procedure of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD is shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, under high-energy electron beam radiation, H2O can be rapidly ionized to produce reductive radicals (eg. eaq – and H·) and oxidative radicals (eg. OH· and H2O2). IPA is used to eliminate oxidative radicals. Under the action of the strong reductive radicals eaq – and H·, SeO3 2– is reduced to Se2–, as shown in Scheme 1a. Then, Zn2+ and Se2– in the organic amine (en) system form organic–inorganic hybrid ZnSe(en)0.5 precursor ultrathin nanosheets. In the meantime, abundant Se vacancies are formed under the action of high-energy electrons. A three-dimensional periodic structure model of ZnSe(en)0.5 with Se vacancies is established by VESTA, as shown in Scheme 1b[37]. Subsequently, ZnSe(en)0.5 as the precursor is carbonized, resulting in the formation of porous ultrathin ZnSe@NC nanosheets. In this process, Se vacancies are still retained in the established ZnSe atomic layer structure model (Scheme 1c), while organic amine (en) is converted to in situ grown nitrogen-doped amorphous carbon. Finally, N-GQDs are loaded on the surface of ZnSe@NC nanosheets by electrodeposition to fabricate Se-vacancy porous ultrathin ZnSe@NC/N-GQD nanosheets. Scheme 1d schematically illustrates the sodiation process, and the improvement of sodium storage performance by the design of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD is described. The distinctive nanostructure of ultrathin nanosheet can reduce the length of electron and ion transport paths, and the porous structure enables buffering of volume expansion, which enhances Na+ diffusion kinetics and cycling stability. Abundant Se vacancies serve to enhance number of active sites available for Na+ storage. In-situ grown nitrogen-doped amorphous carbon derived from ZnSe(en)0.5 precursor allows electrons to transfer freely within them, resulting in enhanced reaction kinetics. The N-GQDs loaded on ZnSe@NC further increase the active sites for Na+ storage and electron conductivity, resulting in enhanced specific capacitance and accelerated reaction kinetics, respectively. In order to illustrate the effect of N-GQDs on sodium storage properties, ZnSe@NC is prepared for comparative analysis.

Fig. 1 a shows the TEM image of the organic–inorganic hybrid ZnSe(en)0.5 precursor. The morphology exhibits leaf-like 2D nanosheets with a length and width of 1.0–1.4 μm and 0.4–0.6 μm, respectively. It can be observed that the edge portion of the nanosheets is nearly transparent, exhibiting the properties of ultrathin nanosheets. The lateral view of the TEM image and AFM image are employed to further investigate the thickness of the nanosheets. The lateral view of the TEM image (Fig. 1b) shows the thickness of ZnSe(en)0.5 nanosheets ranging from 3.00 nm to 4.56 nm. AFM image as presented in Fig. 1c displays a thickness of 3.66 nm, which is consistent with TEM results and shows the ultrathin nanosheet structure of the ZnSe(en)0.5 hybrid [38]. Specifically, as shown in Fig. 1d, ZnSe(en)0.5 nanosheet is composed of uniformly distributed nanoparticles with an average size of about 3.0 nm without agglomeration. Fig. 1e shows the HRTEM of ZnSe(en)0.5 nanosheet. It can be clearly seen that 0.18, 0.1, and 0.33 nm correspond to the (135), (133) and (200) planes of ZnSe(en)0.5 [39]. The SAED pattern (Fig. 1f) can be ascribed to (135), (133) and (200) planes, respectively.

The crystal structure of ZnSe(en)0.5 is identified by XRD. The diffraction peaks in Fig. 2 a are consistent with ZnSe(en)0.5 in the previous reports [39]. In particular, the broad diffraction peaks indicate small particle size, which is corroborated by the size distribution in Fig. 1d. It can be attributed to the rapid nucleation during crystal growth due to electron beam irradiation. To obtain information about the content of en in the ZnSe(en)x, a TGA experiment was conducted, and the result is illustrated in Fig. 2b. The slight weight loss observed at around 100℃ is due to the evaporation of adsorbed water on the surface of the sample [40]. The TGA curve reveals a weight loss of approximately 17.4 % from 200 to 400 °C, which is consistent with the theoretical content of en in the ZnSe(en)0.5 compound (17.2 %) [40,41]. Therefore, the coordination number of organic en can be considered to be 0.5. As shown in Fig. 2c, a signal with a g-value of 2.003 is observed in the EPR spectrum, which corresponds to the unpaired electrons induced by the partial removal of Se [42]. This result indicates that the generation of Se vacancy is induced by electron beam irradiation. Fig. 2d shows the Raman spectrum of ZnSe(en)0.5. The Raman peaks at 209 cm−1 and 254 cm−1 correspond to the transverse optical phonon mode (2TO) peak and longitudinal optical phonon mode (LO) peak of ZnSe, respectively [43]. Both Raman peaks exhibit broad characteristics, which can be attributed to the high surface-to-volume ratio of small particles [43–46]. In addition, an evident TO mode peak is observed at 144 cm−1 (1TO), which may be associated with Se vacancy [47]. This result is consistent with the appearance of the Se vacancy signal peak in Fig. 2c.

Using ZnSe(en)0.5 as the precursor, carbon-coated porous ultrathin ZnSe nanosheets with Se vacancy (ZnSe@NC) are prepared via the pyrolysis carbonization process at 600 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere. TEM image (Fig. 3 a) of the as-prepared ZnSe@NC shows ultrathin nanosheet morphology with numerous pores. The lateral view of TEM image (Fig. 3b) indicates that the thickness ranges from 9.25 nm to 13.74 nm. The corresponding AFM image displays that the thickness of ZnSe@NC is about 15.97 nm (Fig. 3c). Compared with the precursor, the increase in thickness is due to the regrowth of ZnSe crystals during the carbonization process, but the morphology is still ultrathin nanosheets. With the carbonization of en, the porous nanostructure is formed. As shown in Fig. 3d, the nanosheets are constituted of nanoparticles with an average size of 15.17 nm. Due to the decomposition of the ZnSe(en)0.5 precursor, a large number of pores are obviously formed. This porous ultrathin nanosheet structure is beneficial to the diffusion kinetics of Na+ and the cycling stability of SIBs. Typical crystalline features corresponding to the hexagonal ZnSe structure can be observed in HRTEM image of ZnSe@NC (Fig. 3e). Lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.18, 0.20, and 0.24 nm are consistent with the (103), (110), and (102) planes of hexagonal ZnSe (JCPDS No.15–0105). It can be seen that the amorphous carbon with 0.35 nm lattice stripes corresponds to the (002) of carbon. The formation of amorphous carbon can be attributed to the pyrolysis of en, which favors the improvement of electrical conductivity. SAED pattern in Fig. 3f can be ascribed to (103), (110), and (102) planes of hexagonal ZnSe, consistent with the results in Fig. 3e.

The composition of ZnSe@NC was determined by XRD in Fig. 4 a. The peaks are in accordance with the hexagonal ZnSe (JCPDS No.15–0105) and no impurities are present. The porous characteristic of ZnSe@NC was investigated by N2 adsorption–desorption isothermal. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the isotherms curve exhibits the type IV isotherm with the type H3 hysteresis curve [48,49]. The specific surface area (SSA) of ZnSe@NC is determined to be 36.360 m2 g−1 (Table S1). Large SSA is confirmed by the TEM image in Fig. 3d. The pore size distribution range from 1 nm to 62 nm is calculated from the BJH model. In the inset of Fig. 4b, micropores are in the minority, while mesoporous and macropores are in the majority. Table S1 presents the pore volume and average pore diameter of ZnSe@NC, which are 0.144 cm3 g−1 and 16.016 nm, respectively. The large specific surface area and porous structure contribute to the increase of Na+ adsorption sites and diffusion kinetics, as well as the buffering of volume expansion during charge and discharge. The EPR spectrum of ZnSe@NC, as shown in Fig. 4c, indicates the existence of Se vacancy with a clear signal peak at g = 2.003 [42]. The abundant Se vacancies provide more active sites for ZnSe@NC to participate in the reaction, which effectively enhances the Na+ storage performance.

Fig. 4d shows the XPS survey spectrum of ZnSe@NC, which mainly includes the peaks of C, N, Zn, and Se. Fig. S1a displays the C 1 s spectrum with the peaks at 286.11 eV and 284.80 eV attributing to C-N and C-C, indicating the presence of en-derived N element. In the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4e), the peaks at 401.35 eV, 399.97 eV, and 398.51 eV correspond to graphite N, pyrrolic N, and pyridine N, respectively. N-doped carbon with many active sites enhances charge transport and ion adsorption [50]. The Zn 2p spectrum (Fig. 4f) exhibits two peaks at 1044.00 eV and 1020.97 eV, which are attributed to 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 of Zn2+. The Se vacancy causes the nearby Zn atoms to become unsaturated, resulting in the appearance of a new pair of Zn 2p peaks [51]. The peaks at 1045.55 eV and 1022.39 eV indicate the presence of Se vacancy defect, which is consistent with the EPR spectrum in Fig. 4c. The binding energies of 55.02 eV and 53.97 eV in Fig. S1b correspond to Se 3d2/3 and 3d5/2 of Se2– in ZnSe@NC, respectively.

To further enhance the Na+ storage performance, N-GQDs are loaded on ZnSe@NC via electrodeposition to fabricate ZnSe@NC/N-GQD. The synthesized N-GQDs exhibit good dispersion (Fig. S2a) with average size of 3.46 nm (Fig. S2b, c). After loading N-GQDs, the morphology of ultrathin porous nanosheets is preserved, and TEM image of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD has no obvious changes (Fig. 5 a). The lattices observed in the HRTEM image (Fig. 5b) correspond to the (101), (110), and (202) plane of hexagonal ZnSe with spacing of 0.30, 0.20, and 0.15 nm, respectively. Concurrently, lattice stripe with spacing of 0.21 nm is discernible, which correlates with the (100) plane of N-GQDs. The presence of pores is evident in the orange dashed area of the nanosheets (Fig. 5c). In addition, discernible ripples and corrugations of lattice stripes are visible within the pink dashed box. The inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) was employed to furnish more comprehensive data regarding the lattice fringing in this region (Fig. 5d). The lattice fringes corresponding to the (101) plane spacing of 0.30 nm of ZnSe are observed in the curled edges. The presence of discontinuous lattice streaks indicates the presence of a high density of defects. The elemental mapping analysis of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD reveals the atomic proportion and homogeneous distribution of C, N, Zn, and Se (Fig. 5e and Fig. S3). As illustrated in Fig. S4a, the hexagonal crystal structure of ZnSe is preserved in the XRD spectra of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD. Meanwhile, the emergence of a broad peak at approximately 26° is indicative of the loading of N-GQDs, confirming the successful preparation of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD [36]. In Fig. S4b, Raman spectroscopy was employed to analyze the carbon present in ZnSe@NC and ZnSe@NC/N-GQD. The bands at 1347 cm−1 and 1587 cm−1 are attributed to the defects of the lattice and the graphitization degree of carbon, respectively. The ID/IG value of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD is lower than that of ZnSe@NC, indicating a higher degree of graphitization in ZnSe@NC/N-GQD, which can be attributed to the electrodeposition of N-GQDs and the subsequent annealing process [52]. The increased graphitization facilitates rapid charge transfer during the reaction, enhancing electrochemical performance [53]. The EPR spectra of ZnSe@NC and ZnSe@NC/N-GQD as shown in Fig. S5, and the intensity of Se vacancy in ZnSe@NC/N-GQD is lower than that in ZnSe@NC. The abundant Se vacancies remaining in the ZnSe@NC/N-GQD provide more active sites to participate in the reaction, which effectively enhances the Na+ storage performance.

The Na+ storage properties of ZnSe@NC and ZnSe@NC/N-GQD were evaluated in half cells. Fig. 6 a shows the CV curves of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 for 1st to 5th cycles. In the initial cathodic scan, the peaks observed at 0.49 V and 1.61 V can be attributed to the conversion/alloying reaction of ZnSe and the formation of a solid electrolyte interface (SEI) [54]. During the initial anodic scan, the oxidation peaks at 0.81 V, 1.03 V, 1.22 V, and 1.52 V are indicative of the dealloying reaction and subsequent formation of ZnSe from Zn [6]. A pair of peaks at 0.04 V and 0.07 V can be attributed to the sodiation/desodiation process in carbon [55,56]. The oxidation peak at 2.04 V and reduction peak at 1.92 V are attributed to side reactions of Se [57]. The CV curves of ZnSe@NC exhibit similar characteristics to ZnSe@NC/N-GQD, as illustrated in Fig. S6a. In the subsequent cycle, the CV curves exhibit a high degree of overlap with the reduction peaks shift to higher voltage, which indicates excellent reversibility. The involved electrochemical reactions may be described as follows:
(1)
ZnSe + 2 N
a +

S e

Fig. 6b illustrates the galvanostatic charge–discharge voltage (GCD) curves of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD at the 1st, 2nd, 5th, and 10th cycle with a current density of 0.5 A g−1, respectively. The initial charge and discharge capacities of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD are 524 mA h g−1 and 566 mA h g−1, respectively, with an initial coulombic efficiency (ICE) of 92.59 %. This value is much higher than that of ZnSe@NC (66.17 %), as shown in Fig. S6b. The formation of the SEI film and the decomposition of electrolytes result in the occurrence of partial irreversible side reactions, which also leads to the significant capacity attenuation observed in the first cycle [58]. Compared with ZnSe@NC (Fig. S6b), the GCD curves of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD exhibit a higher and more stable capacity after the first cycle, which demonstrates that the deposition of N-GQDs can effectively increase the capacity, prevent irreversible side reactions, and impart the material excellent electrochemical stability. In Fig. 6c, the cycling performance of ZnSe@NC and ZnSe@NC/N-GQD at 0.5 A g−1 is compared. Notably, the discharge capacity of ZnSe@NC shows a significant downward trend, consistent with the GCD in Fig. S6b. After 100 cycles, the capacity exhibits a gradual increase, reaching a high reversible discharge capacity of 410 mA h g−1. In contrast, ZnSe@NC/N-GQD exhibits a higher reversible discharge capacity (483 mA h g−1) with a capacity retention of 95 % (compared to the 2nd cycle). These results suggest that the deposition of N-GQDs can effectively ameliorate the substantial fluctuations in capacity and enhance the reversible capacity. As shown in Fig. 6d, ZnSe@NC/N-GQD exhibits enhanced rate performance at different current densities from 0.05 to 10.0 A g−1. At current densities of 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, and 10.0 A g−1, the specific capacities of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD are 529, 518, 501, 478, 462, 445, 414, and 381 mA h g−1, respectively. A slight decline in capacity is observed when the current density returns to 0.05 A g−1, indicating excellent rate performance and reversibility of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) can be used to further analyze its superior rate performance. Fig. 6e displays the Nyquist plots of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD and ZnSe@NC. Impedance parameters including the internal resistance (R s), electrolyte interface resistance (R SEI), charge-transfer resistance (R ct) and the Warburg impedance (Z w) are calculated from equivalent circuit model (inset Fig. 6e) [6,59–61]. As can be seen from Table S2, R s, R SEI, R ct and Z w of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD are all smaller than ZnSe@NC, indicating that ZnSe@NC/N-GQD has faster kinetics and better rate performance. As shown in Fig. 6f, the rate performance of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD is superior to those of previous reports on ZnSe-based SIBs anodes [6,11,50,54,56,57,59,62–64]. Fig. 6g illustrates the long-term cycling performance at a current density of 5.0 A g−1. After 2800 cycles, ZnSe@NC/N-GQD exhibits a high capacity of 361 mA h g−1 with a capacity retention rate of 86 %, and a Coulombic efficiency (CE) of nearly 100 %, demonstrating highly stable long-cycle performance. In contrast, the cycling curve of ZnSe@NC exhibits a marked decline and subsequent recovery, eventually causing the cell to fail at approximately 700 cycles. In comparison with other ZnSe-based anode materials for SIBs reported previously, ZnSe@NC/N-GQD exhibits higher specific capacity and better cycling stability (Table S3). The morphologies and impedance changes of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD after 1000 cycles at 5.0 A g−1 were investigated. After 1000 cycles, the TEM image shows that the porous ultrathin nanosheet structure of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD remains intact without structural collapse or severe pulverization (Fig. S7a, b), indicating the stability of structural and reversibility of cycling. Moreover, the EIS of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD before and after cycling is presented in Fig. S7c. The result shows that the impedance increases after the cycle, which may be attributed to the volume expansion that occurred after a long cycle.

As shown in Fig. 6c, g, the specific capacity of ZnSe@NC and ZnSe@NC/N-GQD is decreased and then increased gradually. In order to explain this phenomenon, it is necessary to deeply understand the reactions that occur during the cycle. Therefore, we use GCD (Fig. S8 a, c) and dQ/dV curves (Fig. S8b, d) to analyze the REDOX reactions that occur during charging and discharging [65]. As illustrated in the dQ/dV curves (Fig. S8b, d), peak position and the intensity of the redox peaks change during the cycling process for both ZnSe@NC and ZnSe@NC/N-GQD samples. As shown dQ/dV curve of ZnSe@NC in Fig. S8b, in the initial 10 cycles, the oxidation peak at 1.01 V and the reduction peaks at 0.56 V can be observed, which corresponds to the conversion/alloying reaction of ZnSe and the formation of a SEI film. However, continuing to 50 cycles, the intensity of the two peaks drops dramatically, indicating less REDOX reaction, resulting in the lowest capacity at 50 cycles, which can be contributed to a large amount of Na+ irreversibly trapped into the lattice and the formation of SEI film, which fails the subsequent conversion/alloying reaction, resulting in the decrease of discharge capacity [6,66]. As cycling continues from 50 to 200 cycles, the intensity of new oxidation peaks at 1.48 V and 1.80 V and new reduction peaks at 0.69 V, 1.04 V, and 1.62 V increases, indicating that more REDOX reactions occur, which leads to the gradual increase of the capacities. Two oxidation peaks are the result of the generation of Cu2-xSe after cycling using copper foil as the negative current collector, which is a common phenomenon in metal chalcogenide electrodes [67]. These new REDOX peaks are attributed to the sodiation/desodiation reactions of Cu2-xSe [68]. For ZnSe@NC/N-GQD, the capacity also decreases first and then increases. However, compared with ZnSe@NC, due to the action of quantum dots, ZnSe@NC/N-GQD has smaller capacity attenuation range and shorter electrochemical activation time. As shown in Fig. S8d, the REDOX peaks corresponding to Cu2-xSe appear as early as 20 cycles, suggesting that the introduction of N-GQDs promotes the rapid transition and activation process from ZnSe to Cu2-xSe, which may be attributed to the GQDs promoting the formation of a thin SEI layer [69] and/or providing more active sites, enhancing the electrochemical performance.

To investigate the Na+ storage mechanism of ZnSe@NC and ZnSe@NC/N-GQD, the CV curves with different scan rates are shown in Fig. S9a, b. The oxidation and reduction peaks exhibit a consistent change trend with the increase of the scanning rate, and a slight displacement indicates the polarization of the electrode material in the electrolyte [70]. The peak current (i) and scan rate (v) obey the following equation [71]:
(4)
i = a ∙
v
b
where a and b are constants, the b value can be employed to assess the storage behavior of Na+. When b is close to 1.0, it indicates that the electrochemical storage process is mainly capacitance-controlled. Conversely, b near 0.5 indicates that the process is dominated by diffusion-controlled. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a and Fig. S9c, the fitted b values for the reduction peak (Peak 1) and oxidation peak (Peak 2) of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD are 0.76 and 0.86, respectively, and b values of ZnSe@NC are 0.77 and 0.67, respectively. These results indicate that both electrochemical reactions of these two materials are simultaneously capacitance-controlled and diffusion-controlled. ZnSe@NC/N-GQD electrode exhibits superior surface capacitance-controlled storage properties, with more REDOX reaction sites on the surface, resulting in enhanced electrochemical performance.

To further study the process of Na+ storage behavior, the capacitance and diffusion-controlled contribution are quantitatively calculated, and the formula is as follows [72,73]:

corresponds to diffusion-controlled process. The result in Fig. 7b shows that the capacitance-controlled contribution of ZnSe@NC increases from 41 % to 75 % with the scan rate from 0.2 mV s−1 to 1.0 mV s−1, and the contribution of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD increases from 73 % to 91 % with the scan rate from 0.2 mV s−1 to 1.0 mV s−1. In Fig. S10a, b, the capacitive contribution in total charge storage of ZnSe@NC and ZnSe@NC/N-GQD at 0.8 mV s−1 is 74 % and 86 %, respectively. The capacitance-controlled contribution of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD is larger than that of ZnSe@NC at the same sweep rate. This indicates that the enhanced kinetics for Na+ storage can be attributed to the loading of N-GQDs, which improves the rate performance [74]. In addition, the galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) was performed to analyze the Na+ diffusion coefficient (DNa +) during charging and discharging (Fig. 7c). As shown in Fig. 7d, the DNa + values of the ZnSe@NC/N-GQD during discharge are in the range of 10–15.06 to 10–13.23 cm2 s−1, which are higher than those of ZnSe@NC (10–15.50 to 10–13.45 cm2 s−1). Similarly, the DNa + values of the ZnSe@NC/N-GQD during charge range from 10–14.69 to 10–13.26 cm2 s−1, which are also higher than those of ZnSe@NC (10–14.72 to 10–13.53 cm2 s−1). These results are due to the surface modification of N-GQDs with high activity, which effectively enhances the adsorption of Na+ and the electrical conductivity.

The excellent Na+ storage performance of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD is demonstrated by the schematic illustration of electron transport and ion diffusion paths during the de/sodiation process in Fig. 8 . Firstly, the distinctive nanostructure of porous ultrathin nanosheet can reduce the length of electron and ion transport paths, and alleviate the volume expansion, which enhances Na+ diffusion kinetics and achieves long-cycle stability. Secondly, abundant Se vacancies lead to the enhancement of a large number of Na+ storage active sites and reaction kinetics. Thirdly, in-situ grown nitrogen-doped amorphous carbon allows electrons to transfer freely within it, which is conducive to improving the conductivity. Finally, the loading of N-GQDs further enhances the conductivity, and increases REDOX reaction sites and Na+ adsorption sites, resulting in accelerated reaction kinetics, which is conducive to improving the capacity, rate performance, and cycle stability.

In summary, Organic-inorganic hybrid ZnSe(en)0.5 ultrathin nanosheets with abundant Se vacancies are rapidly prepared by electron beam irradiation for the first time. Using it as a precursor, a unique Se-vacancy carbon-coated porous ultrathin ZnSe nanosheets loaded with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots are synthesized via carbonization and electrodeposition process. Benefiting from ultrathin nanosheet morphology, porous nanostructure, abundant Se vacancies, in-situ grown amorphous carbon, and N-GQDs carbon composite, ZnSe@NC/N-GQD exhibits excellent sodium storage properties with a high capacity of 483 mA h g−1 after 200 cycles at 0.5 A g−1, a good rate capability of 381 mA h g−1 at 10.0 A g−1, and an excellent cyclic ability of 86 % retention after 2800 cycles at 5.0 A g−1. Kinetic analysis reveals the mainly capacitive control behavior in Na+ storage mechanism of ZnSe@NC/N-GQD. The GITT analysis reveals the rapid kinetics and higher Na+ diffusion coefficients after the loading of N-GQDs. We believe that this work proposes a new method to rapidly and massively prepare organic–inorganic nanohybrids, and construct Se-vacancy porous ultrathin ZnSe nanosheet/carbon composite as a promising anode material for sodium-ion batteries.
